articleRecords.label: Publication Information
articleRecords.title: Publication Information
articleRecords.summary: NIST AI 100-1 Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0) This publication is available free of charge from: https://doi.org/10.6028/NIST.AI.100-1 January 2023 U.S.

articleRecords.label: Commercial Identification Disclaimer
articleRecords.title: Commercial Identification Disclaimer
articleRecords.summary: Certain commercial entities, equipment, or materials may be identified in this document in order to describe an experimental procedure or concept adequately.

articleRecords.label: Update Schedule and Versions
articleRecords.title: Update Schedule and Versions
articleRecords.summary: The Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework (AI RMF) is intended to be a living document.

articleRecords.label: Table of Contents and Lists of Tables and Figures
articleRecords.title: Table of Contents and Lists of Tables and Figures
articleRecords.summary: Table of Contents Executive Summary 1 Part 1: Foundational Information 4 1 Framing Risk 4 1.1 Understanding and Addressing Risks, Impacts, and Harms 4 1.2 Challenges for AI Risk Management 5 1.2.1 Risk Measurement 5 1.2.2 Risk Tolerance 7 1.2.3 Risk Prioritization 7 1.2.4 Organizational Integration and Management of Risk 8 2 Audience 9 3 AI Risks and…

articleRecords.label: Part 1
articleRecords.title: Part 1: Foundational Information
articleRecords.summary: Part 1: Foundational Information

articleRecords.label: Executive Summary
articleRecords.title: Executive Summary
articleRecords.summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) technologies have significant potential to transform society and people’s lives – from commerce and health to transportation and cybersecurity to the environment and our planet.

articleRecords.title: Framing Risk
articleRecords.summary: AI risk management offers a path to minimize potential negative impacts of AI systems, such as threats to civil liberties and rights, while also providing opportunities to maximize positive impacts.

articleRecords.title: Understanding and Addressing Risks, Impacts, and Harms
articleRecords.summary: In the context of the AI RMF, risk refers to the composite measure of an event’s probability of occurring and the magnitude or degree of the consequences of the corresponding event.

articleRecords.title: Figure 1
articleRecords.summary: Fig.

articleRecords.title: Challenges for AI Risk Management
articleRecords.summary: Several challenges are described below.

articleRecords.title: Risk Measurement
articleRecords.summary: AI risks or failures that are not well-defined or adequately understood are difficult to measure quantitatively or qualitatively.

articleRecords.title: Risk Tolerance
articleRecords.summary: While the AI RMF can be used to prioritize risk, it does not prescribe risk tolerance.

articleRecords.title: Risk Prioritization
articleRecords.summary: Attempting to eliminate negative risk entirely can be counterproductive in practice because not all incidents and failures can be eliminated.

articleRecords.title: Organizational Integration and Management of Risk
articleRecords.summary: AI risks should not be considered in isolation.

articleRecords.title: Audience
articleRecords.summary: Identifying and managing AI risks and potential impacts – both positive and negative – requires a broad set of perspectives and actors across the AI lifecycle.

articleRecords.title: Figure 2
articleRecords.summary: Fig.

articleRecords.title: Figure 3
articleRecords.summary: Fig.

articleRecords.title: AI Risks and Trustworthiness
articleRecords.summary: For AI systems to be trustworthy, they often need to be responsive to a multiplicity of criteria that are of value to interested parties.

articleRecords.title: Figure 4
articleRecords.summary: Fig.

articleRecords.title: Valid and Reliable
articleRecords.summary: Validation is the “confirmation, through the provision of objective evidence, that the requirements for a specific intended use or application have been fulfilled” (Source: ISO 9000:2015).

articleRecords.title: Safe
articleRecords.summary: AI systems should “not under defined conditions, lead to a state in which human life, health, property, or the environment is endangered” (Source: ISO/IEC TS 5723:2022).

articleRecords.title: Secure and Resilient
articleRecords.summary: AI systems, as well as the ecosystems in which they are deployed, may be said to be resilient if they can withstand unexpected adverse events or unexpected changes in their environment or use – or if they can maintain their functions and structure in the face of internal and external change and degrade safely and gracefully when this is necessary (Adapted…

articleRecords.title: Accountable and Transparent
articleRecords.summary: Trustworthy AI depends upon accountability.

articleRecords.title: Explainable and Interpretable
articleRecords.summary: Explainability refers to a representation of the mechanisms underlying AI systems’ operation, whereas interpretability refers to the meaning of AI systems’ output in the context of their designed functional purposes.

articleRecords.title: Privacy-Enhanced
articleRecords.summary: Privacy refers generally to the norms and practices that help to safeguard human autonomy, identity, and dignity.

articleRecords.title: Fair – with Harmful Bias Managed
articleRecords.summary: Fairness in AI includes concerns for equality and equity by addressing issues such as harmful bias and discrimination.

articleRecords.title: Effectiveness of the AI RMF
articleRecords.summary: Evaluations of AI RMF effectiveness – including ways to measure bottom-line improvements in the trustworthiness of AI systems – will be part of future NIST activities, in conjunction with the AI community.

articleRecords.label: Part 2
articleRecords.title: Part 2: Core and Profiles
articleRecords.summary: Part 2: Core and Profiles

articleRecords.title: AI RMF Core
articleRecords.summary: The AI RMF Core provides outcomes and actions that enable dialogue, understanding, and activities to manage AI risks and responsibly develop trustworthy AI systems.

articleRecords.title: Figure 5
articleRecords.summary: Fig.

articleRecords.title: Govern
articleRecords.summary: The GOVERN function: cultivates and implements a culture of risk management within organizations designing, developing, deploying, evaluating, or acquiring AI systems; outlines processes, documents, and organizational schemes that anticipate, identify, and manage the risks a system can pose, including to users and others across society – and procedures to…

articleRecords.title: Table 1: GOVERN categories and subcategories
articleRecords.summary: Table 1: Categories and subcategories for the GOVERN function.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 1
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 1
articleRecords.summary: Policies, processes, procedures, and practices across the organization related to the mapping, measuring, and managing of AI risks are in place, transparent, and implemented effectively.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 1.1
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 1.1
articleRecords.summary: Legal and regulatory requirements involving AI are understood, managed, and documented.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 1.2
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 1.2
articleRecords.summary: The characteristics of trustworthy AI are integrated into organizational policies, processes, procedures, and practices.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 1.3
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 1.3
articleRecords.summary: Processes, procedures, and practices are in place to determine the needed level of risk management activities based on the organization's risk tolerance.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 1.4
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 1.4
articleRecords.summary: The risk management process and its outcomes are established through transparent policies, procedures, and other controls based on organizational risk priorities.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 1.5
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 1.5
articleRecords.summary: Ongoing monitoring and periodic review of the risk management process and its outcomes are planned and organizational roles and responsibilities clearly defined, including determining the frequency of periodic review.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 1.6
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 1.6
articleRecords.summary: Mechanisms are in place to inventory AI systems and are resourced according to organizational risk priorities.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 1.7
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 1.7
articleRecords.summary: Processes and procedures are in place for decommissioning and phasing out AI systems safely and in a manner that does not increase risks or decrease the organization’s trustworthiness.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 2
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 2
articleRecords.summary: Accountability structures are in place so that the appropriate teams and individuals are empowered, responsible, and trained for mapping, measuring, and managing AI risks.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 2.1
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 2.1
articleRecords.summary: Roles and responsibilities and lines of communication related to mapping, measuring, and managing AI risks are documented and are clear to individuals and teams throughout the organization.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 2.2
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 2.2
articleRecords.summary: The organization’s personnel and partners receive AI risk management training to enable them to perform their duties and responsibilities consistent with related policies, procedures, and agreements.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 2.3
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 2.3
articleRecords.summary: Executive leadership of the organization takes responsibility for decisions about risks associated with AI system development and deployment.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 3
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 3
articleRecords.summary: Workforce diversity, equity, inclusion, and accessibility processes are prioritized in the mapping, measuring, and managing of AI risks throughout the lifecycle.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 3.1
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 3.1
articleRecords.summary: Decision-making related to mapping, measuring, and managing AI risks throughout the lifecycle is informed by a diverse team (e.g., diversity of demographics, disciplines, experience, expertise, and backgrounds).

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 3.2
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 3.2
articleRecords.summary: Policies and procedures are in place to define and differentiate roles and responsibilities for human-AI configurations and oversight of AI systems.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 4
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 4
articleRecords.summary: Organizational teams are committed to a culture that considers and communicates AI risk.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 4.1
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 4.1
articleRecords.summary: Organizational policies and practices are in place to foster a critical thinking and safety-first mindset in the design, development, deployment, and uses of AI systems to minimize potential negative impacts.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 4.2
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 4.2
articleRecords.summary: Organizational teams document the risks and potential impacts of the AI technology they design, develop, deploy, evaluate, and use, and they communicate about the impacts more broadly.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 4.3
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 4.3
articleRecords.summary: Organizational practices are in place to enable AI testing, identification of incidents, and information sharing.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 5
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 5
articleRecords.summary: Processes are in place for robust engagement with relevant AI actors.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 5.1
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 5.1
articleRecords.summary: Organizational policies and practices are in place to collect, consider, prioritize, and integrate feedback from those external to the team that developed or deployed the AI system regarding the potential individual and societal impacts related to AI risks.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 5.2
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 5.2
articleRecords.summary: Mechanisms are established to enable the team that developed or deployed AI systems to regularly incorporate adjudicated feedback from relevant AI actors into system design and implementation.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 6
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 6
articleRecords.summary: Policies and procedures are in place to address AI risks and benefits arising from third-party software and data and other supply chain issues.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 6.1
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 6.1
articleRecords.summary: Policies and procedures are in place that address AI risks associated with third-party entities, including risks of infringement of a third-party’s intellectual property or other rights.

articleRecords.label: GOVERN 6.2
articleRecords.title: GOVERN 6.2
articleRecords.summary: Contingency processes are in place to handle failures or incidents in third-party data or AI systems deemed to be high-risk.

articleRecords.title: Map
articleRecords.summary: The MAP function establishes the context to frame risks related to an AI system.

articleRecords.title: Table 2: MAP categories and subcategories
articleRecords.summary: Table 2: Categories and subcategories for the MAP function.

articleRecords.label: MAP 1
articleRecords.title: MAP 1
articleRecords.summary: Context is established and understood.

articleRecords.label: MAP 1.1
articleRecords.title: MAP 1.1
articleRecords.summary: Intended purposes, potentially beneficial uses, context-specific laws, norms and expectations, and prospective settings in which the AI system will be deployed are understood and documented.

articleRecords.label: MAP 1.2
articleRecords.title: MAP 1.2
articleRecords.summary: Interdisciplinary AI actors, competencies, skills, and capacities for establishing context reflect demographic diversity and broad domain and user experience expertise, and their participation is documented.

articleRecords.label: MAP 1.3
articleRecords.title: MAP 1.3
articleRecords.summary: The organization’s mission and relevant goals for AI technology are understood and documented.

articleRecords.label: MAP 1.4
articleRecords.title: MAP 1.4
articleRecords.summary: The business value or context of business use has been clearly defined or – in the case of assessing existing AI systems – re-evaluated.

articleRecords.label: MAP 1.5
articleRecords.title: MAP 1.5
articleRecords.summary: Organizational risk tolerances are determined and documented.

articleRecords.label: MAP 1.6
articleRecords.title: MAP 1.6
articleRecords.summary: System requirements (e.g., “the system shall respect the privacy of its users”) are elicited from and understood by relevant AI actors.

articleRecords.label: MAP 2
articleRecords.title: MAP 2
articleRecords.summary: Categorization of the AI system is performed.

articleRecords.label: MAP 2.1
articleRecords.title: MAP 2.1
articleRecords.summary: The specific tasks and methods used to implement the tasks that the AI system will support are defined (e.g., classifiers, generative models, recommenders).

articleRecords.label: MAP 2.2
articleRecords.title: MAP 2.2
articleRecords.summary: Information about the AI system’s knowledge limits and how system output may be utilized and overseen by humans is documented.

articleRecords.label: MAP 2.3
articleRecords.title: MAP 2.3
articleRecords.summary: Scientific integrity and TEVV considerations are identified and documented, including those related to experimental design, data collection and selection (e.g., availability, representativeness, suitability), system trustworthiness, and construct validation.

articleRecords.label: MAP 3
articleRecords.title: MAP 3
articleRecords.summary: AI capabilities, targeted usage, goals, and expected benefits and costs compared with appropriate benchmarks are understood.

articleRecords.label: MAP 3.1
articleRecords.title: MAP 3.1
articleRecords.summary: Potential benefits of intended AI system functionality and performance are examined and documented.

articleRecords.label: MAP 3.2
articleRecords.title: MAP 3.2
articleRecords.summary: Potential costs, including non-monetary costs, which result from expected or realized AI errors or system functionality and trustworthiness – as connected to organizational risk tolerance – are examined and documented.

articleRecords.label: MAP 3.3
articleRecords.title: MAP 3.3
articleRecords.summary: Targeted application scope is specified and documented based on the system’s capability, established context, and AI system categorization.

articleRecords.label: MAP 3.4
articleRecords.title: MAP 3.4
articleRecords.summary: Processes for operator and practitioner proficiency with AI system performance and trustworthiness – and relevant technical standards and certifications – are defined, assessed, and documented.

articleRecords.label: MAP 3.5
articleRecords.title: MAP 3.5
articleRecords.summary: Processes for human oversight are defined, assessed, and documented in accordance with organizational policies from the GOVERN function.

articleRecords.label: MAP 4
articleRecords.title: MAP 4
articleRecords.summary: Risks and benefits are mapped for all components of the AI system including third-party software and data.

articleRecords.label: MAP 4.1
articleRecords.title: MAP 4.1
articleRecords.summary: Approaches for mapping AI technology and legal risks of its components – including the use of third-party data or software – are in place, followed, and documented, as are risks of infringement of a third party’s intellectual property or other rights.

articleRecords.label: MAP 4.2
articleRecords.title: MAP 4.2
articleRecords.summary: Internal risk controls for components of the AI system, including third-party AI technologies, are identified and documented.

articleRecords.label: MAP 5
articleRecords.title: MAP 5
articleRecords.summary: Impacts to individuals, groups, communities, organizations, and society are characterized.

articleRecords.label: MAP 5.1
articleRecords.title: MAP 5.1
articleRecords.summary: Likelihood and magnitude of each identified impact (both potentially beneficial and harmful) based on expected use, past uses of AI systems in similar contexts, public incident reports, feedback from those external to the team that developed or deployed the AI system, or other data are identified and documented.

articleRecords.label: MAP 5.2
articleRecords.title: MAP 5.2
articleRecords.summary: Practices and personnel for supporting regular engagement with relevant AI actors and integrating feedback about positive, negative, and unanticipated impacts are in place and documented.

articleRecords.title: Measure
articleRecords.summary: The MEASURE function employs quantitative, qualitative, or mixed-method tools, techniques, and methodologies to analyze, assess, benchmark, and monitor AI risk and related impacts.

articleRecords.title: Table 3: MEASURE categories and subcategories
articleRecords.summary: Table 3: Categories and subcategories for the MEASURE function

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 1
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 1
articleRecords.summary: Appropriate methods and metrics are identified and applied.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 1.1
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 1.1
articleRecords.summary: Approaches and metrics for measurement of AI risks enumerated during the MAP function are selected for implementation starting with the most significant AI risks.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 1.2
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 1.2
articleRecords.summary: Appropriateness of AI metrics and effectiveness of existing controls are regularly assessed and updated, including reports of errors and potential impacts on affected communities.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 1.3
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 1.3
articleRecords.summary: Internal experts who did not serve as front-line developers for the system and/or independent assessors are involved in regular assessments and updates.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 2
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 2
articleRecords.summary: AI systems are evaluated for trustworthy characteristics.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 2.1
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 2.1
articleRecords.summary: Test sets, metrics, and details about the tools used during TEVV are documented.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 2.2
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 2.2
articleRecords.summary: Evaluations involving human subjects meet applicable requirements (including human subject protection) and are representative of the relevant population.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 2.3
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 2.3
articleRecords.summary: AI system performance or assurance criteria are measured qualitatively or quantitatively and demonstrated for conditions similar to deployment setting(s).

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 2.4
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 2.4
articleRecords.summary: The functionality and behavior of the AI system and its components – as identified in the MAP function – are monitored when in production.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 2.5
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 2.5
articleRecords.summary: The AI system to be deployed is demonstrated to be valid and reliable.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 2.6
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 2.6
articleRecords.summary: The AI system is evaluated regularly for safety risks – as identified in the MAP function.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 2.7
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 2.7
articleRecords.summary: AI system security and resilience – as identified in the MAP function – are evaluated and documented.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 2.8
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 2.8
articleRecords.summary: Risks associated with transparency and accountability – as identified in the MAP function – are examined and documented.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 2.9
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 2.9
articleRecords.summary: The AI model is explained, validated, and documented, and AI system output is interpreted within its context – as identified in the MAP function – to inform responsible use and governance.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 2.10
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 2.10
articleRecords.summary: Privacy risk of the AI system – as identified in the MAP function – is examined and documented.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 2.11
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 2.11
articleRecords.summary: Fairness and bias – as identified in the MAP function – are evaluated and results are documented.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 2.12
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 2.12
articleRecords.summary: Environmental impact and sustainability of AI model training and management activities – as identified in the MAP function – are assessed and documented.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 2.13
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 2.13
articleRecords.summary: Effectiveness of the employed TEVV metrics and processes in the MEASURE function are evaluated and documented.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 3
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 3
articleRecords.summary: Mechanisms for tracking identified AI risks over time are in place.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 3.1
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 3.1
articleRecords.summary: Approaches, personnel, and documentation are in place to regularly identify and track existing, unanticipated, and emergent AI risks based on factors such as intended and actual performance in deployed contexts.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 3.2
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 3.2
articleRecords.summary: Risk tracking approaches are considered for settings where AI risks are difficult to assess using currently available measurement techniques or where metrics are not yet available.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 3.3
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 3.3
articleRecords.summary: Feedback processes for end users and impacted communities to report problems and appeal system outcomes are established and integrated into AI system evaluation metrics.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 4
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 4
articleRecords.summary: Feedback about efficacy of measurement is gathered and assessed.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 4.1
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 4.1
articleRecords.summary: Measurement approaches for identifying AI risks are connected to deployment context(s) and informed through consultation with domain experts and other end users.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 4.2
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 4.2
articleRecords.summary: Measurement results regarding AI system trustworthiness in deployment context(s) and across the AI lifecycle are informed by input from domain experts and relevant AI actors to validate whether the system is performing consistently as intended.

articleRecords.label: MEASURE 4.3
articleRecords.title: MEASURE 4.3
articleRecords.summary: Measurable performance improvements or declines based on consultations with relevant AI actors, including affected communities, and field data about context-relevant risks and trustworthiness characteristics are identified and documented.

articleRecords.title: Manage
articleRecords.summary: The MANAGE function entails allocating risk resources to mapped and measured risks on a regular basis and as defined by the GOVERN function.

articleRecords.title: Table 4: MANAGE categories and subcategories
articleRecords.summary: Table 4: Categories and subcategories for the MANAGE function.

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 1
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 1
articleRecords.summary: AI risks based on assessments and other analytical output from the MAP and MEASURE functions are prioritized, responded to, and managed

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 1.1
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 1.1
articleRecords.summary: A determination is made as to whether the AI system achieves its intended purposes and stated objectives and whether its development or deployment should proceed.

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 1.2
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 1.2
articleRecords.summary: Treatment of documented AI risks is prioritized based on impact, likelihood, and available resources or methods.

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 1.3
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 1.3
articleRecords.summary: Responses to the AI risks deemed high priority, as identified by the MAP function, are developed, planned, and documented.

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 1.4
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 1.4
articleRecords.summary: Negative residual risks (defined as the sum of all unmitigated risks) to both downstream acquirers of AI systems and end users are documented.

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 2
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 2
articleRecords.summary: Strategies to maximize AI benefits and minimize negative impacts are planned, prepared, implemented, documented, and informed by input from relevant AI actors.

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 2.1
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 2.1
articleRecords.summary: Resources required to manage AI risks are taken into account – along with viable non-AI alternative systems, approaches, or methods – to reduce the magnitude or likelihood of potential impacts.

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 2.2
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 2.2
articleRecords.summary: Mechanisms are in place and applied to sustain the value of deployed AI systems.

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 2.3
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 2.3
articleRecords.summary: Procedures are followed to respond to and recover from a previously unknown risk when it is identified.

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 2.4
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 2.4
articleRecords.summary: Mechanisms are in place and applied, and responsibilities are assigned and understood, to supersede, disengage, or deactivate AI systems that demonstrate performance or outcomes inconsistent with intended use.

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 3
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 3
articleRecords.summary: AI risks and benefits from third-party entities are managed.

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 3.1
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 3.1
articleRecords.summary: AI risks and benefits from third-party resources are regularly monitored, and risk controls are applied and documented.

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 3.2
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 3.2
articleRecords.summary: Pre-trained models which are used for development are monitored as part of AI system regular monitoring and maintenance.

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 4
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 4
articleRecords.summary: Risk treatments, including response and recovery, and communication plans for the identified and measured AI risks are documented and monitored regularly.

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 4.1
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 4.1
articleRecords.summary: Post-deployment AI system monitoring plans are implemented, including mechanisms for capturing and evaluating input from users and other relevant AI actors, appeal and override, decommissioning, incident response, recovery, and change management.

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 4.2
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 4.2
articleRecords.summary: Measurable activities for continual improvements are integrated into AI system updates and include regular engagement with interested parties, including relevant AI actors.

articleRecords.label: MANAGE 4.3
articleRecords.title: MANAGE 4.3
articleRecords.summary: Incidents and errors are communicated to relevant AI actors, including affected communities.

articleRecords.title: AI RMF Profiles
articleRecords.summary: AI RMF use-case profiles are implementations of the AI RMF functions, categories, and subcategories for a specific setting or application based on the requirements, risk tolerance, and resources of the Framework user: for example, an AI RMF hiring profile or an AI RMF fair housing profile.

articleRecords.label: Appendix A: Descriptions of AI Actor Tasks from Figures 2 and 3
articleRecords.title: Appendix A: Descriptions of AI Actor Tasks from Figures 2 and 3
articleRecords.summary: AI Design tasks are performed during the Application Context and Data and Input phases of the AI lifecycle in Figure 2.

articleRecords.label: Additional AI actors
articleRecords.title: Additional AI actors
articleRecords.summary: Third-party entities include providers, developers, vendors, and evaluators of data, algorithms, models, and/or systems and related services for another organization or the organization’s customers or clients.

articleRecords.label: Appendix B: How AI Risks Differ from Traditional Software Risks
articleRecords.title: Appendix B: How AI Risks Differ from Traditional Software Risks
articleRecords.summary: As with traditional software, risks from AI-based technology can be bigger than an enterprise, span organizations, and lead to societal impacts.

articleRecords.label: Appendix C: AI Risk Management and Human-AI Interaction
articleRecords.title: Appendix C: AI Risk Management and Human-AI Interaction
articleRecords.summary: Organizations that design, develop, or deploy AI systems for use in operational settings may enhance their AI risk management by understanding current limitations of human-AI interaction.

articleRecords.label: Appendix D: Attributes of the AI RMF
articleRecords.title: Appendix D: Attributes of the AI RMF
articleRecords.summary: NIST described several key attributes of the AI RMF when work on the Framework first began.

seedProvisions.title: Principles relating to processing of personal data
seedProvisions.summary: States the GDPR's core processing principles, expressly including lawfulness, fairness, purpose limitation, data minimisation, storage limitation, security, and accountability.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in gdpr-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: Concept tags identify express principles in the Article; they do not establish equivalence with another instrument.

seedProvisions.title: Lawfulness of processing
seedProvisions.summary: Identifies the conditions under which processing is lawful, including consent and five other legal bases, and sets a compatibility analysis for certain further processing.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in gdpr-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: Consent is one legal basis among several; this concept tag does not present it as the default basis.

seedProvisions.title: Processing of special categories of personal data
seedProvisions.summary: Prohibits processing specified special categories of personal data unless one of the Article's enumerated conditions and safeguards applies.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in gdpr-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: The GDPR's enumerated special categories should not be treated as a universal definition of sensitive data.

seedProvisions.title: Transparent information, communication and modalities for the exercise of the rights of the data subject
seedProvisions.summary: Sets transparency, communication, timing, identity-verification, and procedural requirements for notices and the exercise of GDPR data-subject rights.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in gdpr-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: The Article supplies modalities for specified GDPR rights rather than a jurisdiction-neutral request procedure.

seedProvisions.title: Right of access by the data subject
seedProvisions.summary: Gives data subjects a qualified right to confirmation, access, specified processing information, and a copy of personal data undergoing processing.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in gdpr-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: Scope, verification, copies, third-party rights, and applicable restrictions require provision-specific analysis.

seedProvisions.title: Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’)
seedProvisions.summary: Creates a qualified right and corresponding controller duty to erase personal data when stated grounds apply, subject to enumerated exceptions.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in gdpr-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: The right is conditional and subject to exceptions; the tag does not imply an absolute deletion requirement.

seedProvisions.title: Data protection by design and by default
seedProvisions.summary: Requires risk-sensitive technical and organisational measures that implement data-protection principles in system design and ensure necessary-only processing by default.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in gdpr-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: Pseudonymisation is an example measure in the Article, not a universal safe harbor or complete compliance solution.

seedProvisions.title: Processor
seedProvisions.summary: Governs controller selection and oversight of processors, processor contracts, subprocessor authorization, assistance, return or deletion, and audit support.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in gdpr-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: The supply-chain tag is thematic; controller-processor roles and duties must be established under the GDPR itself.

seedProvisions.title: Automated individual decision-making, including profiling
seedProvisions.summary: Provides protections concerning certain decisions based solely on automated processing that produce legal or similarly significant effects, subject to stated exceptions and safeguards.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in gdpr-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: Summary does not reproduce statutory text or resolve CJEU interpretation.

seedProvisions.title: Security of processing
seedProvisions.summary: Requires controllers and processors to implement security measures appropriate to risk, informed by context, cost, and the likelihood and severity of impacts.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in gdpr-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: The listed measures are contextual rather than a universal control checklist.

seedProvisions.title: Notification of a personal data breach to the supervisory authority
seedProvisions.summary: Requires controllers to notify the competent supervisory authority of qualifying personal-data breaches, generally within 72 hours, and creates related processor and documentation duties.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in gdpr-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: Risk exceptions and national supervisory practice require context.

seedProvisions.title: Data protection impact assessment
seedProvisions.summary: Requires an impact assessment before processing that is likely to result in a high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms, including listed categories of processing.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in gdpr-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: Supervisory-authority lists and guidance can change the practical trigger analysis.

seedProvisions.title: General principle for transfers
seedProvisions.summary: Conditions transfers of personal data to third countries or international organizations on compliance with GDPR Chapter V while preserving the Regulation's level of protection.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in gdpr-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: A complete transfer analysis also requires the other provisions of Chapter V and current case law.

seedProvisions.title: Risk management system
seedProvisions.summary: Requires an iterative lifecycle risk-management system for high-risk AI systems within the Article's scope.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in eu-ai-act-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: Status is recorded two weeks before the general application date; Article 113 exceptions must be checked.

seedProvisions.title: Data and data governance
seedProvisions.summary: Sets quality and governance requirements for training, validation, and testing data sets used by high-risk AI systems, subject to its scope and exceptions.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in eu-ai-act-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: Requirements are specific to scoped high-risk systems and are not a general data-protection rule.

seedProvisions.title: Transparency and provision of information to deployers
seedProvisions.summary: Requires high-risk AI systems to be sufficiently transparent for deployers to interpret outputs and use the system appropriately, with instructions containing specified information.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in eu-ai-act-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: Do not conflate provider-to-deployer transparency with an individual right to explanation.

seedProvisions.title: Accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity
seedProvisions.summary: Requires high-risk AI systems to achieve appropriate levels of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity and to perform consistently throughout their lifecycle.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in eu-ai-act-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: The applicable state of the art and system context remain material.

seedProvisions.title: Responsibilities along the AI value chain
seedProvisions.summary: Allocates provider responsibilities when specified parties modify or rebrand high-risk AI systems and requires cooperation and written arrangements with certain third-party suppliers.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in eu-ai-act-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: The thematic supply-chain tag does not erase the Article's actor-specific conditions, exceptions, or allocation rules.

seedProvisions.title: Transparency obligations for providers and deployers of certain AI systems
seedProvisions.summary: Creates scenario-specific duties to inform people when they interact with AI and to mark or disclose specified synthetic or manipulated content, subject to the Article's exceptions and implementation rules.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in eu-ai-act-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: The Article contains actor-, system-, content-, and context-specific exceptions; the concept tag is not a universal disclosure rule.

seedProvisions.title: Post-market monitoring by providers and post-market monitoring plan for high-risk AI systems
seedProvisions.summary: Requires proportionate, documented post-market monitoring that systematically gathers and analyses performance data throughout a high-risk AI system's lifetime to evaluate continuing compliance.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in eu-ai-act-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: Monitoring duties are scoped to covered systems and must be read with the Act's implementation and transition rules.

seedProvisions.title: Entry into force and application
seedProvisions.summary: Establishes the AI Act's entry into force, general application date, and the principal earlier and later application exceptions.

textAvailability.note: A complete official Article record is generated in eu-ai-act-articles.json.

source.label: EUR-Lex

editorial.note: The visualization should derive provision status from this phased schedule rather than a single instrument-wide boolean.

seedProvisions.title: Reporting for dual-use foundation models and computing clusters
seedProvisions.summary: Directed use of Defense Production Act authorities to require specified reports and information concerning certain dual-use foundation models, large computing clusters, and associated testing.

textAvailability.note: Historical text remains available from the Federal Register.

source.label: Federal Register

editorial.note: No current obligation should be inferred after revocation of the parent order.

seedProvisions.title: Developing an Artificial Intelligence Action Plan
seedProvisions.summary: Directed designated White House officials, in coordination with relevant agencies, to develop an action plan implementing the order's AI leadership policy.

textAvailability.note: Official text is linked from the Federal Register.

source.label: Federal Register

editorial.note: The resulting Action Plan and later executive orders are distinct instruments.

seedProvisions.title: Automated decision-making safeguards
seedProvisions.summary: Requires transparency, fairness, and impartiality in automated decision-making and provides specific protections concerning marketing and decisions with a major impact on personal rights and interests.

textAvailability.note: Official Chinese Article text is stored verbatim; the English title and summary remain editorial.

source.label: Cyberspace Administration of China

editorial.note: Terms and enforcement practice should be checked in Chinese-language authorities.

seedProvisions.title: Mechanisms for providing personal information outside China
seedProvisions.summary: Lists legal mechanisms for overseas provision of personal information, including security assessment, certification, standard-contract, and other authorized routes.

textAvailability.note: Official Chinese Article text is stored verbatim; subordinate transfer rules and the editorial English summary remain separate.

source.label: Cyberspace Administration of China

editorial.note: Do not infer that listed routes operate identically to GDPR Chapter V mechanisms.

seedProvisions.title: Personal-information security measures
seedProvisions.summary: Requires personal-information handlers to adopt measures suited to purpose, method, category, impact, and security risk, including governance and technical controls.

textAvailability.note: Official Chinese Article text is stored verbatim; the English title and summary remain editorial.

source.label: Cyberspace Administration of China

editorial.note: Control lists and risk standards may also be specified elsewhere in Chinese law and standards.

seedProvisions.title: Personal-information protection impact assessment
seedProvisions.summary: Requires prior personal-information protection impact assessments for listed higher-impact activities, including sensitive information, automated decisions, entrusted processing, and overseas provision.

textAvailability.note: Official Chinese Article text is stored verbatim; Article 56 and the editorial English summary remain separate.

source.label: Cyberspace Administration of China

editorial.note: Assessment content and retention are further addressed in Article 56.

seedProvisions.title: Personal-information incident response
seedProvisions.summary: Requires immediate remedial measures and notice to the responsible authority and affected individuals when personal information is or may be leaked, altered, or lost, subject to stated conditions.

textAvailability.note: Official Chinese Article text is stored verbatim; the English title and summary remain editorial.

source.label: Cyberspace Administration of China

editorial.note: No fixed GDPR-style 72-hour deadline should be inferred from this summary.

seedProvisions.title: Algorithmic impact assessment

seedProvisions.locator: AI management system requirements — high-level overview

seedProvisions.summary: Frames AI governance as an organization-wide management system with defined responsibilities, repeatable processes, evaluation, corrective action, and continual improvement for AI-related risks and opportunities.
